metaTitle: Child Custody Law Bangladesh 2026 – Complete Guide
title: Child Custody Law in Bangladesh 2026: Rights, Courts, and Recent Updates
metaDescription: A complete 2026 guide to child custody law in Bangladesh — Muslim law, Hindu law, court procedure, best interests of the child, and how to file a custody case.
heroIntro: Child custody disputes are among the most emotionally charged and legally complex matters in Bangladesh's family courts. Whether you are a mother asserting your right of hizanat (custody) or a father seeking guardianship over your children, understanding the legal framework in 2026 is the first step toward protecting your parental rights. This guide covers the laws, court procedures, recent judicial trends, and practical tips for parents navigating custody proceedings in Bangladesh.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Child Custody in Bangladesh 2026
points: Under Muslim law, mothers have the right of hizanat (physical custody) for sons up to age 7 and daughters until puberty.
Under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890, courts apply the 'welfare of the child' principle as the paramount consideration.
Fathers are the natural guardians of their children's person and property under Muslim law.
Either parent can file a Guardianship petition before the Family Court under the Family Courts Ordinance 1985.
Courts increasingly consider the child's own wishes, especially for children over 7 years of age.

sections.h2: Overview of Child Custody Law in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Child custody in Bangladesh is governed by a combination of personal law (based on the religion of the parties), statutory law, and general equitable principles applied by the courts. The primary legal frameworks are:

  • Muslim Family Law: For Muslim parents (who constitute the majority of the population), the rules of hizanat (custody) derived from Islamic jurisprudence apply, as recognised and developed by Bangladesh courts.
  • Guardians and Wards Act, 1890: A general statute applicable to all citizens that empowers courts to appoint guardians and make custody orders based on the welfare of the child.
  • Family Courts Ordinance, 1985: Provides the procedural framework for family disputes, including custody matters, before the Family Court (which is the Court of the Senior Assistant Judge at the district level).
  • Hindu Law: For Hindu parents, customary rules and the general welfare principle apply.
  • Special Marriages Act & Other Personal Laws: Apply to marriages under other religious or civil frameworks.

In 2026, Bangladesh courts continue to apply both personal law rules and the overriding welfare of the child standard. Where personal law rules appear to conflict with a child's best interests, courts have consistently held that the child's welfare takes precedence — a principle affirmed by the Appellate Division of the Supreme Court in numerous landmark judgments.

Under classical Islamic law as applied in Bangladesh, the mother has the primary right of hizanat — the physical care and upbringing of young children. The rules are as follows:

  • Sons: The mother is entitled to custody of male children until the age of 7 years.
  • Daughters: The mother is entitled to custody of female children until they reach puberty (typically around 13–15 years).
  • After these ages: The father (as natural guardian) is generally entitled to custody, though courts may deviate from this rule in the child's interest.

The mother's right of hizanat is forfeited in specific circumstances under Muslim law:

  • If the mother remarries a person who is not a mahram (a close relative forbidden to marry the child);
  • If the mother is guilty of gross immoral conduct or neglect;
  • If the mother lives far away and relocation would harm the child;
  • If the mother is found unfit by the court on any ground affecting the child's welfare.

Bangladesh courts have increasingly departed from a rigid application of the age-based rule and instead focus on which parent can best serve the child's overall welfare — physical, emotional, educational, and moral. For guidance on your specific situation, consult a qualified family lawyer in Bangladesh.

sections.h2: Father's Rights: Natural Guardianship Under Muslim Law
sections.content:
While the mother holds the right of physical custody (hizanat) for young children, the father is recognised as the natural guardian of the child's person and property under Muslim law. This distinction is important:

  • The mother's custody relates to day-to-day physical care, upbringing, and nurturing.
  • The father's guardianship relates to legal decisions concerning the child — education, religion, travel, medical treatment, and management of property.

The father's rights as natural guardian include:

  • The right to determine the child's religion and education;
  • The right to manage and protect the child's property;
  • The right to give the child in marriage (for daughters);
  • The right to apply for the child's passport and travel documents;
  • The right to custody after the hizanat period ends.

Even during the mother's period of hizanat, the father has the right to visitation (mulaqat). Courts regularly make visitation orders specifying the schedule for the non-custodial parent to spend time with the child. Denial of court-ordered visitation is a contempt of court.

If you are a father seeking custody or greater involvement with your children, a divorce and family lawyer can help you file the appropriate application and argue your case effectively.

sections.h2: The Guardians and Wards Act 1890: Court's Role
sections.content:
The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 empowers courts to appoint a guardian of the person or property of a minor when it is for the welfare of the minor to do so. Key provisions include:

  • Section 7: The court may make an order declaring a person to be the guardian of a minor's person, property, or both.
  • Section 17: In appointing or declaring a guardian, the court shall be guided by what appears to be for the welfare of the minor — considering the minor's age, sex, religion, character and capacity of the proposed guardian, and wishes of deceased parents or any existing custody arrangement.
  • Section 25: The court may order that the ward (child) be returned to the custody of the guardian if wrongfully removed.
  • Section 26: Provides for the variation or revocation of a guardianship order if circumstances change.

The Family Court (Senior Assistant Judge's Court) at the district level has jurisdiction over guardianship applications under the Guardians and Wards Act, as read with the Family Courts Ordinance 1985. In practice, custody disputes in family court proceed on an expedited basis to protect children from prolonged uncertainty.

sections.h2: How to File a Child Custody Case in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Filing a child custody case in Bangladesh follows these steps:

  1. Consult a Family Lawyer: Engage an experienced divorce and family lawyer who can evaluate your case, advise on your rights, and prepare the application.
  2. Prepare the Petition: Draft a Guardianship Petition (under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890) or a custody application under the Family Courts Ordinance 1985 setting out the facts of your marriage, the child's particulars, the current custody situation, and why the court should grant you custody.
  3. File at Family Court: File the petition at the Family Court in the district where the child resides. Pay the required court fee.
  4. Court Issues Notice: The court issues notice to the other parent (respondent) requiring them to appear and file a written statement in response.
  5. Interim Custody Order: In urgent cases (e.g., if the child has been illegally removed), you can apply for an interim custody order at the first hearing, which the court may grant pending final determination.
  6. Evidence and Hearing: Both parties present evidence — documentary and oral witnesses. The court may also interview the child in chambers if the child is old enough to express a preference.
  7. Decree: The court passes a final decree on custody, visitation, and maintenance.
  8. Appeal: If aggrieved by the Family Court's decision, you can appeal to the District Judge's Court and then to the High Court Division.

sections.h2: Factors Courts Consider When Deciding Custody
sections.content:
Bangladesh courts apply the welfare principle as the paramount standard, considering a broad range of factors:

  • Age and sex of the child: Young children and girls receive special consideration for maternal custody.
  • Physical and mental health of parents: A parent with serious illness or mental health issues may not be granted custody.
  • Economic stability: The court examines the financial capacity of each parent to provide for the child's education, healthcare, and upbringing.
  • Moral character: Evidence of substance abuse, criminal history, or immoral conduct is taken seriously.
  • Existing bond with the child: The strength of the emotional relationship between the child and each parent is considered.
  • Child's preference: For children over 7 years, the court gives weight to the child's expressed preference, though this is not determinative.
  • Continuity and stability: Courts are reluctant to disrupt an existing stable arrangement unless there is a compelling reason.
  • Sibling relationships: Keeping siblings together is generally preferred unless circumstances dictate otherwise.

sections.h2: International Child Abduction and Cross-Border Custody
sections.content:
Cases where one parent takes the child abroad without the other parent's consent or a court order are particularly complex. Bangladesh is not a signatory to the Hague Convention on International Child Abduction, which limits international legal remedies.

However, available remedies in Bangladesh include:

  • Obtaining a prohibitory injunction from the High Court preventing the child's removal from Bangladesh;
  • Filing a habeas corpus petition before the High Court Division under Article 102 of the Constitution to order the production and return of the child;
  • Placing the child's name on a port alert (immigration watch list) through the court to prevent departure;
  • Filing a criminal complaint for child abduction under the Penal Code 1860 if the circumstances warrant.

If your child has been taken abroad, you should immediately consult a Supreme Court lawyer in Bangladesh who can file emergency applications before the High Court Division. Time is critical in such cases.

sections.h2: 2026 Judicial Trends in Bangladesh Custody Cases
sections.content:
In 2026, Bangladesh family courts continue to evolve their approach to child custody, reflecting both changes in social norms and judicial guidance from the higher courts. Key trends include:

  • Child-Centred Approach: Courts increasingly focus on the child's own expressed wishes and psychological wellbeing, going beyond rigid religious law age-limits.
  • Joint Parenting Orders: While formal joint custody is uncommon in Bangladesh, courts are making more detailed visitation orders that effectively allow both parents meaningful involvement in the child's life.
  • Use of Child Psychologists: Higher courts have begun appointing expert assessors or child psychologists in complex cases to assist the court in understanding the child's needs and preferences.
  • Strict Action Against Denial of Visitation: Courts are increasingly willing to impose contempt sanctions on parents who deny the other parent court-ordered visitation rights.
  • Mediation Encouraged: Family courts now actively encourage mediation as a first step in custody disputes to reach amicable settlements that spare the child the trauma of contested proceedings.

faqs.question: Can a mother lose custody of her child in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. A mother can lose her right of hizanat if she remarries a stranger (someone not a close relative of the child), is found guilty of gross misconduct or neglect, becomes unfit for custody, or if the court finds that the child's welfare is better served by the father. Courts always apply the welfare of the child as the primary test.

faqs.question: At what age can a child choose which parent to live with in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: There is no fixed statutory age. However, courts typically begin giving weight to a child's preference from around age 7. The older and more mature the child, the more weight is given to their expressed wishes. The court may interview the child in chambers to ascertain their views.

faqs.question: Can a father get custody of a young child under 7 years old?
faqs.answer: Generally, the mother has the right of hizanat for sons under 7 years. However, if the mother is found unfit, is unavailable, or the court finds that the child's welfare is better served by the father, the court can grant custody to the father even for a child under 7. The welfare of the child overrides strict age-based rules.

faqs.question: How long does a child custody case take in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: A custody case before the Family Court typically takes 1 to 3 years to fully conclude, depending on the complexity of the matter and court delays. However, interim custody orders can be obtained within the first few hearings (weeks) to provide immediate relief.

faqs.question: What is the difference between custody and guardianship in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Custody refers to the physical care and day-to-day upbringing of the child (hizanat), which is primarily the mother's right for young children. Guardianship refers to the legal authority over the child's person and property, including decisions about education, religion, travel, and marriage — which is primarily the father's domain under Muslim law. Both concepts can be adjudicated by the court.
